Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd just drift through the day doing other things. Most of my 18 hours of studying happened in bursts, usually late at night when anxiety kicked in about upcoming deadlines. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time watching videos and making mental maps, but organizing when to do all that? Not my strong suit.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really dependent on deadlines. I'd go days without touching course material, then suddenly panic and cram everything in. When I had assignments due, I'd be up late trying to catch up on weeks of content. Regular schedules just don't stick for me, even though I know they'd probably help with my anxiety.
Q3: Usually it was panic, to be honest. I'd remember an assignment was coming up or see a notification and feel that wave of dread. Sometimes the gamified tasks actually pulled me in because they felt less overwhelming than regular assignments. Other times I'd log in just to see what I'd missed, which often made me more anxious when I saw how behind I was.
Q4: The gamified elements actually helped make logging in feel less like a chore sometimes. When they introduced those point systems and achievements, I found myself checking in more regularly just to see my progress. It became almost like a game I'd play, which made it easier to handle. But that routine was fragile—one bad week and I'd fall off completely.
Q5: The videos were my lifeline. I watched like 139 minutes worth because I could actually see concepts being explained, pause, rewind, really absorb it visually. The gamified tasks also felt useful because they broke things down into smaller, less terrifying pieces. When something has a progress bar and rewards, it doesn't feel as overwhelming as a massive assignment.
Q6: Discussion posts and peer interactions—I barely touched those. I only made one post all semester. Social stuff online stresses me out; I worry about saying something wrong or looking stupid. Reading materials I'd often skip if there was a video alternative. If something felt optional and text-heavy, it went to the bottom of my priority list and usually stayed there.
Q7: If it felt achievable and had some kind of immediate feedback, I was more likely to do it. The gamified stuff worked because I could see points adding up. Activities that felt endless or unclear about what success looked like? Those I'd avoid. With my anxiety, I need to know I can actually finish something without spiraling.
Q8: Required stuff carried this weight of obligation that made me anxious but eventually forced action. Optional activities felt like nice ideas I'd never get to because I was always behind on the required ones. There's this guilt with optional stuff too—like I should be doing it but can't bring myself to start. Required deadlines at least gave me a clear enemy to fight against.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download videos and PDFs to my laptop because my internet isn't always reliable with my limited resources. I'd study from screenshots of diagrams on my phone while on the bus. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS—just me with downloaded materials, trying to piece things together visually in my head.
Q10: There were whole evenings where I'd be reviewing downloaded lecture slides, drawing concept maps on paper, really trying to understand the material. None of that showed up anywhere. I'd watch a video once online, then replay it five times from my downloads. The LMS only saw that first view, not the hours of offline review that actually helped me learn.
Q11: All the time. I'd download everything I could in one session when my internet was working well, then spend days working through it offline. My exam score was decent because of that offline studying, even though my attendance and completion rates look terrible. The LMS doesn't know I basically memorized those downloaded videos.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety spiked, I felt completely overwhelmed by how much I'd fallen behind, and my resilience is basically nonexistent. When things pile up, I freeze instead of acting. There were weeks where just thinking about logging in made my chest tight, so I'd avoid it entirely, which only made everything worse.
Q13: Absolutely. Being low on resources means I'm always juggling—work, family stuff, limited internet time. When I'm exhausted, the LMS feels like this mountain I can't climb. Confusion about what was actually required versus optional made me waste time on wrong things. Fatigue plus anxiety equals paralysis for me.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd be logged in, scrolling through materials, but my brain was somewhere else entirely—worrying about other things, feeling too anxious to actually absorb anything. I'd watch a video but realize at the end I hadn't processed a single thing. Present in the system, absent in my head.
Q15: Constantly. I'd know an assignment was important but the anxiety of starting it would be so intense that I'd do anything else instead. It's not laziness—it's like this wall goes up between me and the task. The bigger or more important something seemed, the harder it was to begin. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, every single day.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that actually made me act. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely. But they also caused massive stress spikes—I'd be inactive for weeks, then frantically active in the last 48 hours. My LMS usage probably looks like a heart monitor with huge gaps and sudden peaks. Not healthy, but it's how I functioned.
Q17: When I got good feedback on my assignment scores, it actually motivated me for a bit. Seeing that 88 on assignments made me think maybe I could do this. But if feedback was delayed or critical, I'd spiral and avoid the course for days. My mood is really tied to how I think I'm performing, so feedback hits hard either way.
Q18: The course having 20 online modules felt overwhelming from the start. I'd look at the list and feel defeated before beginning. The gamified elements helped break things up, but the overall structure felt like too much for someone like me who struggles to stay organized. Clearer weekly chunks might have helped me feel less lost.
Q19: The gamification definitely made engagement easier—points and progress bars speak to my visual brain. But the sheer volume of content and the lack of clear prioritization made it harder. I never knew what was truly essential versus nice-to-have. When everything seems equally important, I can't figure out where to start, so sometimes I just don't.
Q20: That sounds like me sometimes. I'd log in, watch videos, click around, genuinely try to engage—but when it came to actually submitting work, the anxiety would stop me. Starting is one thing; finishing and putting yourself out there for judgment is another. High activity can mean someone's trying hard but hitting invisible walls.
Q21: That also sounds like me, weirdly. My exam score was 76 and I got Merit despite terrible attendance and completion rates. I was learning, just not in ways the system tracked. Downloaded materials, offline studying, visual memorization—none of that shows up. Low LMS activity doesn't mean low learning, just different learning.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked and what you opened, but they can't show what was happening in your head. They miss offline studying, anxiety paralysis, genuine effort that doesn't result in submissions. They capture the skeleton of engagement but miss the flesh—the struggle, the understanding, the invisible work.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through motions. It's when I'm genuinely curious, when concepts click and I want to learn more. It's not about how many times I logged in—it's about those moments when my brain is fully present and working, even if I'm just staring at a downloaded PDF on my phone.
Q24: The LMS can't see my anxiety attacks before assignments, the hours of offline studying, or how the gamified tasks actually made me feel capable sometimes. It can't see that I care about learning even when my behavior looks like I don't. It misses that I'm fighting against my own brain just to show up at all, and sometimes that fight is invisible.
